1: ki hu hayah gibbor milchamah (gibbor, H1368; milchamah, H4421): 'for he was a man of war.' Manasseh is the firstborn of Joseph, and his firstborn son Machir is specifically noted as 'a man of war' — the military reputation that earned him the trans-Jordan allocation of Gilead and Bashan (Num 32:39-40). Gibbor milchamah (warrior/mighty man of war) is a compound title: gibbor denotes exceptional strength or valor, milchamah designates the military context. Machir's military prowess is what qualified his descendants for the most exposed territory — the east side of the Jordan, bordering Ammon and Aram.
2: livnei Menasheh haNivtarim lemishpechotam: 'for the rest of the children of Manasseh by their clans.' The west-bank Manasseh territory is allocated to the remaining clans: Abiezer, Helek, Asriel, Shechem, Hepher, and Shemida — six sons/clans of Manasseh receiving the western half-tribe territory. Shechem (Shekhem, shoulder/ridge) is among these — the strategically located city at the pass between Ebal and Gerizim (the covenant ceremony site of ch8). The name Shechem appears as both a person (Manasseh's descendant) and the city that his descendants presumably occupied — the personal name reflecting the clan's territorial association.
3: uvTzlofchad ben-Chefer lo-hayu lo vanim ki im-vanot (vanot, H1323): 'Zelophehad son of Hepher had no sons but only daughters.' Zelophehad's daughters (Mahlah, Noah, Hoglah, Milcah, Tirzah) return to claim the inheritance established for them in Numbers 27:1-11, when they argued before Moses that their father's portion should not be lost simply because he had no sons. YHWH ruled in their favor — a landmark legal decision in ancient Israel that preserved daughters' inheritance rights in the absence of sons. Their reappearance in Joshua 17 confirms that the Mosaic ruling is now implemented in the actual land allocation.
4: va'tikrevanah lifnei El'azar hakohen (qarav, H7126): 'they drew near before Eleazar the priest.' Qarav (draw near, approach) has both ordinary and liturgical uses — approaching a judge or official, but also drawing near to YHWH in worship. The legal petition before Eleazar the priest (the successor of Aaron) and Joshua replicates the earlier case before Moses and Eleazar in Numbers 27:2. The continuity of the legal procedure — women bringing a test case before the appointed leadership — demonstrates that Joshua's governance followed Mosaic precedent. Ketsivah YHWH et-Mosheh (as YHWH commanded Moses) — the legal basis is the divine precedent.
5: vayiplu chelqei Menasheh asarah (chalaq, H2506; asarah, H6235): 'there fell ten portions to Manasseh.' The addition of Zelophehad's five daughters to the five other Manassite clans produces ten portions — an accounting that demonstrates the daughters' claim is legally equivalent to the clan-heads'. The number ten gives Manasseh the largest allocation among the half-tribes, matching the size of the territory (the fertile central highlands plus the Gilead/Bashan east of the Jordan). The daughters' inheritance is not a concession but a genuine tribal allocation calculated alongside the men's.
6: ki nachu bnot Menasheh bechelev benav: 'for the daughters of Manasseh received an inheritance together with his sons.' The explicit statement of the daughters' equal participation in the inheritance is the narrative's confirmation that the Numbers 27 ruling was fully implemented. Nachalah (inheritance) for the daughters is the same word used throughout for the tribal allocations — not a secondary or lesser form of possession but the same covenantal land-grant. The social innovation of daughters' inheritance rights in ancient Israel — unusual in the ancient Near East — is given permanent place in the land allotment records.
7: vayehi gevul Menasheh me'Asher el-Michmetat: 'the border of Manasseh was from Asher to Michmethath.' Manasseh's western territory borders Asher in the north and extends south to the Ephraim boundary (v.10). The boundary description of west-bank Manasseh is complex because its territory interdigitated with Issachar's and Asher's territories through the system of enclave cities (vv.11-13). The geographical complexity reflects the actual settlement patterns: the Joseph tribes and the northern tribes occupied overlapping territory in the Jezreel Valley region.
8: leMenasheh hayetah eretz Tappuach: 'to Manasseh belonged the land of Tappuah.' The apple-tree town (Tappuah) and its surrounding land is allocated to Manasseh, but the city itself is on Ephraim's border (16:8). The distinction between land (eretz) and city (ir) reflects the ANE administrative reality: territorial control over agricultural land and administrative control of a city could be held by different parties. The city of Tappuah belonged to Ephraim's clans even though the agricultural land around it was Manasseh's — a practical arrangement for a border community.
9: veyarad hagvul nachal Qanah: 'the border goes down to the brook Kanah.' The Kanah wadi (appearing also in Ephraim's boundary, 16:8) forms the natural boundary between the two Joseph tribes in the western lowlands. The same stream serves double duty as a boundary feature for both Ephraim (northern edge) and Manasseh (southern edge). The cities within Manasseh's territory that were among Ephraim's cities (v.9b) represent the same administrative complexity as Ephraim's enclaves in Manasseh (16:9) — a reciprocal arrangement that defies simple territorial mapping.
10: Efrayim mitzafon uleMenasheh mitzafon: 'Ephraim is on the north side, Manasseh is on the north side.' The formula for the complex northern boundary: the territory runs from Asher on the west to Issachar on the east, with both Joseph tribes sharing access. The Sea of Galilee-Jezreel Valley-Carmel range forms the northern boundary of the central territories. Manasseh's territory in the Jezreel Valley plain will be the site of numerous decisive battles in Israel's history: Megiddo (the watchtower of the valley), Jezreel, Beth-shan.
11: vayehi limnasheh bivIssachar uvAsher Beit She'an uchnoteha: 'Manasseh had in Issachar and Asher, Beth-shean and its villages.' The enclave cities of Manasseh within Issachar and Asher territories (vv.11-12) include some of the most strategically important cities in the land: Beth-shean (at the junction of the Jezreel Valley and the Jordan Valley), Ibleam, Dor, En-dor, Taanach, and Megiddo. Beit She'an (House of Rest or House of Shean) was a major Egyptian administrative center — the walls of Saul's body were hung on its walls by the Philistines (1 Sam 31:10-12). The city's strategic value at the valley junction made it perpetually contested.
12: velo-yachlu bnei Menasheh lehorisham (lo-yachlu, H3808+3201; yarash, H3423): 'the children of Manasseh could not dispossess those cities.' The key phrase replicates exactly the Ephraim failure at Gezer (16:10): lo-yachlu lehorisham (they could not dispossess them). The pattern is becoming a structural feature of the northern allocation chapters: for each tribe, the formal allocation is accompanied by the note that certain cities were not taken. Taanach, Megiddo, Beth-shean — the most strategically important cities in the north — remained Canaanite. The unconquered cities are not marginal towns but the dominant urban centers that shaped the political landscape.
13: vayehi ki chazqu bnei Yisrael vayitnu et-haKena'ani lemas (amas, H553; mas, H4522): 'when the Israelites grew strong they put the Canaanites to forced labor but did not drive them out completely.' The pattern: strength used not for complete conquest but for economic exploitation. Chazaq (grew strong) is the same word used for 'be strong and courageous' in ch1 — but here strength produces tolerated servitude rather than obedience to YHWH's command. The repeated mas oved (forced labor) throughout the allotment chapters is the OT's documentation of Israel's systematic compromise: they replaced the commanded cherem (destruction) with the convenient mas (slave labor). The downstream consequences of this compromise fill the book of Judges.
14: madu'a natata li nachalah goral echad (goral, H1486; nachalah, H5159): 'why have you given me only one lot and one portion as an inheritance?' The Joseph tribes' complaint about their allocation is the first recorded dissatisfaction with the land distribution. The grounds: their size (am rav, great/numerous people) and YHWH's blessing (berachtani, he has blessed me). The argument is demographic — a large, blessed people deserves proportionate territory. The complaint is not illegitimate on its face: the Joseph tribes were the largest (Num 1:32-35: Ephraim 40,500; Manasseh 32,200 = 72,700) and their territory was indeed constrained by Canaanite control of the valley cities.
15: im-rav atah aleh lecha haya'ar: 'if you are a great people, go up to the forest.' Joshua's answer is a challenge: if you need more space, create it. Ya'ar (forest/jungle) is the uncultivated hill country that could be cleared for agriculture. Uve'erat lecha sham be'eretz haPerizzi vehaRefaim (clear a place for yourself in the land of the Perizzites and the Rephaim) — two populations known for their presence in the highland wilderness. Joshua's response refuses to simply give them more cultivated land; he points them to the work of clearing the forested highlands. The answer is practical: expansion requires labor, not complaint.
16: veyomer har Efrayim lo yimtza lanu: 'the hill of Ephraim is not enough for us.' The Joseph tribes acknowledge the constraint but press further: the hill country is insufficient, and the valley cities remain Canaanite with their iron chariots (rechev barzel, H7393+1270). Rechev barzel (iron chariots) — the Iron Age military technology that gave Canaanite city-states decisive tactical advantage in the flat valley terrain. Iron chariots could not be effectively countered by infantry on the plains; they were irrelevant in the hill country's rough terrain. The Joseph tribes' complaint reveals their real fear: they cannot take the valley cities by conventional means.
17: am rav atah vekoach gadol lach: 'you are a numerous people and have great power.' Joshua affirms their demographic argument but reframes it: greatness requires corresponding effort, not proportionate handout. Lo-yihyeh lecha goral echad (you shall not have only one lot) — the promise of expansion, but earned, not given. The response models a leadership principle: affirm the legitimate complaint while redirecting energy toward solution rather than continued grievance. Joshua does not dismiss the Joseph tribes' concern but refuses to solve it administratively when the solution requires them to act.
18: ki har yihyeh lach ki ya'ar hu uve'arto vehayah lecha totzatav: 'for the hill country shall be yours, for though it is forested, you shall clear it and its far end shall be yours.' Uve'arto (clear it) — the imperative of active landtaking. Totzatav (its going-out, its far border) — the edge of the cleared land becomes the tribe's new boundary. The vision is expansionary: clearing the forest extends the inheritance, each tree felled marking new territory claimed. ki rechev barzel lo: 'though they have iron chariots.' Joshua acknowledges the military reality of the valley cities and still says the hill country is winnable. The faith-logic of the conquest: YHWH gives the land, but the people must take it — and where human strength is insufficient, YHWH's presence makes the difference (cf. Judg 4-5, where Deborah defeats iron chariots in the same valley).
